question: In a metric space, state the definition of compactness and the definition of sequential compactness.
expectedPoints: Definition of Compactness: A metric space (X,d) is compact if every open cover of X has a finite subcover.
Definition of Sequential Compactness: A metric space (X,d) is sequentially compact if every sequence in X has a convergent subsequence that converges to a point in X.

question: Outline the main steps involved in proving that sequential compactness implies compactness in a metric space.
expectedPoints: Step 1: Show that sequential compactness implies total boundedness.

Step 2: Show that sequential compactness implies the Lebesgue Number Lemma holds (i.e., for any open cover, there exists a δ > 0 such that every set of diameter less than δ is contained in some member of the cover).

Step 3: Use total boundedness to cover the space with finitely many balls of radius δ/2. Each ball, having diameter δ, will lie in some set of the open cover. The corresponding finitely many sets from the cover then form a finite subcover.

question: Prove that in a metric space (X, d), the following are equivalent: (i) X is compact, and (ii) X is sequentially compact. Your proof should cover both directions of the implication.
expectedPoints: Proof that Compactness implies Sequential Compactness: Assume X is compact. Take any sequence (x_n). For contradiction, assume it has no convergent subsequence. Then no point x in X is a limit of a subsequence, so for each x there is an open ball B(x, ε_x) containing only finitely many x_n. These balls form an open cover. By compactness, a finite subcover exists, implying the entire sequence is contained in finitely many sets, each containing finitely many points. This is a contradiction, so a convergent subsequence must exist.

Proof that Sequential Compactness implies Compactness: As outlined in the 5-mark question: (a) Prove that sequential compactness implies total boundedness. If not totally bounded, there exists an ε > 0 for which no finite ε-net exists, allowing the construction of a sequence with all points at least ε apart, which can have no convergent subsequence. (b) Prove that sequential compactness implies the existence of a Lebesgue number for any open cover. If not, for each n, find a set A_n of diameter < 1/n not contained in any cover element. Pick x_n in A_n. By sequential compactness, (x_n) has a convergent subsequence x_{n_k} → x. Since the cover is open, x is in some U. There exists δ > 0 so B(x, δ) ⊂ U. For large k, diam(A_{n_k}) < δ/2 and x_{n_k} is close to x, so A_{n_k} ⊂ B(x, δ) ⊂ U, a contradiction. (c) Use total boundedness and the Lebesgue number δ to create a finite δ/2-net. The balls around these points have diameter δ, so each lies in some U_i of the cover. The finite collection of these U_i is a finite subcover.
